notes.anchor: To him we seem to be uncles
notes.note: A bilingual pun: the Greek theioi means both "uncles" and "divine ones," so to the small boy Cicero and Atticus appear god-like. Atticus was the boy's actual uncle by Cicero's marriage hopes for the families.

notes.anchor: I had in my hands the work
notes.note: One of the antiquarian constitutional treatises of Dicaearchus of Messana, the Peripatetic geographer and political writer (pupil of Aristotle); his works On the Pellenians, On the Corinthians, and On the Athenians described those states' political histories.
Procilius, a Roman antiquarian writer on early Rome and its topography, whom Cicero ranks far below the learned Dicaearchus.

notes.anchor: Herodes, if he were a man
notes.note: Herodes, a Greek rhetorician and man of letters in Atticus's circle who had written attacking Cicero; Cicero jokes he should read Dicaearchus instead of penning such letters.

notes.anchor: I had rather have conspired than have resisted the conspiracy
notes.note: An ironic flourish: Cicero, the man who crushed Catiline's conspiracy in 63 BC, says he would sooner have joined a plot than put up with listening to Herodes.

notes.anchor: do you not see the Kalends coming
notes.note: The Kalends were the first day of the Roman month; the approaching Kalends here mark a court deadline. Cicero invites Atticus to dine "on the day before the Kalends."
C. Antonius Hybrida, Cicero's consular colleague of 63 BC, then facing prosecution (he was convicted in 59); his failure to appear was holding up the court.

notes.anchor: Nigidius is threatening in a public meeting
notes.note: P. Nigidius Figulus, the polymath senator and Pythagorean scholar, then a praetor or court official threatening to compel absent jurors to attend.
